definition: Cross-asset markets are pricing an internally inconsistent labor market narrative. Equities (S&P ~21x forward P/E) price robust consumption at ~4.0% unemployment. Rates (pricing 75-100bp of easing) price enough softening for Fed cuts. These are arithmetically inconsistent without an exogenous positive supply shock. Reverse DCF shows current valuations require either above-target inflation or heroic productivity.
evidence: 50bp real rate move = 12-15% Mag-7 fair value change
Mag-7 effective equity duration ~25-30 years at current multiples
Directly observable in current market data across multiple asset classes
Central bank gold buying at 1,000+ tonnes/year
Pair_0 debate: inconsistency is clear but resolution direction is uncertain
IG spreads ~90-110bp near cycle tights vs ACM term premium at 50-130bp
Different asset classes imply different AI productivity scenarios
pair_2 debate resolved as roughly 60-40 genuine vs artifactual inconsistency
Winner-take-all equilibrium serves as a fragile partial reconciliation
Equity ERP, bond r*, and credit spreads share common input data
open_questions: Fraction attributable to equilibrium winner-take-all vs mispricing remains undetermined
Whether capex/revenue improving 3+ quarters would resolve the inconsistency
Does inconsistency resolve by credit/equity catching up (widening) or rates/gold reverting (compressing)?
What is the historical timeline for resolution of cross-asset inconsistencies of this magnitude?
Is the resolution direction an investment thesis or an analytical finding?
Whether the inconsistency resolves via equities repricing down or rates repricing up
How much of the independent signal is an artifact of shared methodology
Whether winner-take-all equilibrium is stable enough to persist
